coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal carbonization of carboxymethylcellulose: One-pot preparation of conductive carbon microspheres and water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qiong
Li, Wei
Tan, Jia
Wu, Yanjiao
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: Conductive carbon spheres (CSs) and water-soluble fluorescent carbon nano-dots (CNDs) were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) using urea as the nitrogen source. The CSs have diameters of 2–8μm and abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups. They contain N in two forms: (1) chemical N including amidogen and imido groups (NH2 /NH), and (2) structural N including pyridine (N-6), pyrrolic/pyridone (N-5) and quaternary (N-Q) nitrogen. Activation at high temperature contributes to the formation of pyridine (N-6) and quaternary nitrogen (N-Q) with a graphite-like structure and higher stability. They are all conductive and CS0.2-900 was found to exhibit the highest electrical conductivity of 166S/m. CNDs are monodispersed spheres with an average diameter of 32nm and show abundant oxygen-containing groups on their surface. They have excitation- and emission-independent pH-sensitive properties. The use of CNDs as photosensitizer in a CNDs/TiO2 system for methylene blue and phenol degradation under visible light irradiation is attractive. The spectral response range of the system was thus expanded from UV region to include part of the visible light region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal carbonization
Carboxymethylcellulose
Electrical conductivity
Carbon nanodots
Photocatalysts

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Activation of carbon microspheres under a N2 atmosphere
Photocatalytic activity and quantum yield test
Thermal stability and conductivity of the doped materials
Texture and surface properties of the CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of CMC for N-doped carbon spheres and the CNDs.
SEM images of CSs (a) CS0.1 (b) CS0.2 (c) and CS0.4 (d).
O1s and N1s XPS spectra of CSs, CS0.2, CS0.2-550 and CS0.2-900.
XRD spectra of CSs, CS0.2, CS0.2-550 and CS0.2-900.
(a) TEM image (inset shows the corresponding ordered crystal structure) and (b) the size distribution of the CNDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CNDs. Inset is a photograph of the CNDs under ambient light, (b) PL emission and excitation spectra, (c) emission spectra of the CNDs at different excitation wavelengths, and (d) photograph of the CNDs irradiated with a UV lamp at 365nm.

(a) SEM and corresponding TEM (inset) images of the CNDs/TiO2 composites, (b) MB degradation percentage vs time curves over the CNDs/TiO2 and (c) the catalytic mechanism for the CNDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation.
Elemental (wt%) and XPS (wt%) analysis of CSs, CS n and CS n -T.
Surface concentration ratios of the nitrogen and oxygen species (%) obtained by fitting N1s and O1s core level XPS spectra.
Summary of d-spacings for CSs, CS0.2, CS0.2-550 and CS0.2-900.
Specific conductivity of the obtained samples.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal carbonization of carboxymethylcellulose: One-pot preparation of conductive carbon microspheres and water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Northeast Forestry University, P.O. Box 307, 26 Hexing Road, Xiangfang District, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, PR China. Tel./fax: +86 451 82191204.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conductive carbon spheres (CSs) and water-soluble fluorescent carbon nano-dots (CNDs) were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) using urea as the nitrogen source. The CSs have diameters of 2–8μm and abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups. They contain N in two forms: (1) chemical N including amidogen and imido groups (NH2 /NH), and (2) structural N including pyridine (N-6), pyrrolic/pyridone (N-5) and quaternary (N-Q) nitrogen. Activation at high temperature contributes to the formation of pyridine (N-6) and quaternary nitrogen (N-Q) with a graphite-like structure and higher stability. They are all conductive and CS0.2-900 was found to exhibit the highest electrical conductivity of 166S/m. CNDs are monodispersed spheres with an average diameter of 32nm and show abundant oxygen-containing groups on their surface. They have excitation- and emission-independent pH-sensitive properties. The use of CNDs as photosensitizer in a CNDs/TiO2 system for methylene blue and phenol degradation under visible light irradiation is attractive. The spectral response range of the system was thus expanded from UV region to include part of the visible light region.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a novel, convenient and environmentally friendly way to convert biomass into higher value carbonaceous materials. It involves reactions in a closed system under mild temperatures (120–280°C) and self-generated pressure. This avoids traditional harsh conditions such as strong acids, high temperatures, and long reaction times. Much work has been done using glucose, starch, cellulose, and lignocelluloses as bioresource precursors [1–3]. The resultant materials are nanostructured solid carbon microspheres with abundant functional groups on the surface. Therefore, they may be used in environmental and energy fields such as catalysis [4], adsorption [5], as super-capacitors [6] and as electrode materials [7]. HTC can also be used to tune product morphology, structural stability and surface functionality. To the best of our knowledge, both the properties and applications of direct biomass hydrothermal carbonization are simple and scalable.

Doping is an efficient method for the modification of carbon materials and many properties can be changed by changing the texture and the surface by manipulating the doping method and the sources. Most research into doping has focused on N-doping with synthesis being through nitrogen or oxygen rich precursors or by the modification of a carbon surface by introducing N-containing groups, followed by activation [6,8]. It has been reported that this modification of materials shows basic characteristics and that it is effective for the adsorption of acidic materials such as H2S, CO2, and HCl. N-doping can influence the structural arrangement, surface functional groups, stabilities and thermal properties, which further expands the range of applications. The nitrogen source and the doping methods greatly influence the surface groups, the N content and the structure.

Several research groups have investigated, directly or indirectly, the reaction mechanisms of hydrothermal carbonization. A clear reaction pathway has still not been agreed upon, but many reports include chemical compositions, dehydrated intermediates, and the structure of the reaction products. Sevilla and Fuertes [9] used cellulose as a raw material and found that the process involved dehydration, polymerization, aromatization and carbonization. The final solid products consisted of lignin with condensed benzene rings and a large amount of oxygen-containing groups on the core–shell structure. Xiao et al. [2] found that in addition to solid residues (hydrochar), the liquid intermediate products that were extracted with ethyl acetate and analyzed by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry were mainly phenolic compounds and furan derivatives. This confirmed that dehydration and aromatization had occurred. Furthermore, water-soluble compounds in the aqueous solutions from HTC experiments contain a significant amount of sugar and lignin-derived compounds [10], indicating that the aqueous solutions may contain particles with structures and properties similar to that of hydrochar.

Most attention has been given to the solid products obtained to determine the desired structure and the reaction mechanism. The pale yellow liquid has always been regarded as waste and discarded. Research data about the properties and applications of solid carbon microspheres and liquid nano carbon dots is lacking. Our previous research confirmed that the liquid contains abundant carbon nanoparticles similar to quantum dots. They range from a few nanometers to hundreds of nanometers in size with oxygen-containing groups on their surface, and exhibit similar as well as different spectral properties compared with the obtained solid products because of the small size effect [11]. Compared with traditional fluorescent nanoparticles of semiconductor quantum dots [12] and metal nanoparticles [13], these carbon nanodots (CNDs) overcome disadvantages such as toxicity, high-cost, and undesirable oxidation. Additionally, they can be used in metal probes, imaging, optical sensors, catalysis and in energy fields [14–17].

We systematically studied the HTC of CMC with the addition of urea to understand the properties and applications of solid–liquid products that were obtained in the autoclave. Solid carbon spheres with N-containing functional groups and their influence on electrical conductivity were investigated. The photoluminescent liquid products and the visible light catalytic activity of the CNDs/TiO2 system toward methylene blue (MB) and phenol degradation were also investigated.

In a typical experiment, CMC (1.5g) and urea (X g; X =0.1, 0.2, 0.4) were dissolved in distilled water (40ml) and ultrasonically agitated for 15min. The solution was added to a stainless steel autoclave (55ml volume). The autoclave was placed in a furnace at 210°C for 12h, and then allowed to cool to room temperature. The black solid products were collected by centrifugation at 8000rpm and washed with distilled water and pure ethanol until the solution was clear. Drying was done under vacuum at 80°C overnight and the product was designated CS n , where n denotes the mass of added N. Samples without urea were designated CSs.

After obtaining the CS0.2 sample, the corresponding brown solution was collected by further centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 20min. The obtained light yellow solution containing the CNDs was subjected to dialysis (1000Da molecular weight cut off) for about 48h before further characterization. The sample obtained without the addition of urea was designated CNDs-0.
The synthetic strategy is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of carbon microspheres under a N2 atmosphere
ce:section.ce:para: The black solid CS n and CSs samples were placed in an alumina combustion boat which then was placed in a calcination furnace. The sample was kept under a nitrogen atmosphere in the furnace and then heated to 550°C, and 900°C at a rate of 5°Cmin−1. The residence time was 2h after which the furnace was allowed to cool to room temperature. The obtained products were washed with boiling water to remove the excess urea decomposition products. The products after treatment are designated CS n -T where T denotes the temperature of activation.

The CNDs/TiO2 composites were synthesized by the sol–gel method [11]. Tetrabutyl titanate (0.5ml) was dissolved in ethanol (20ml) to form aqueous solution A, and then water (9ml) and ammonia (5ml, 28%) were added leading to aqueous solution B. B was added to A in a dropwise manner with continual stirring. The mixture was left to age for 12h and then dried at 80°C for 6h after centrifugation. TiO2 was prepared by calcining at 500°C for 1h. The above-mentioned TiO2 (0.1g) was added to the CNDs solution (5ml) with stirring over 10min followed by drying before further use.

The morphologies and sizes of the solid products were obtained by environmental scanning electric microscopy (SEM; Quanta 200, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). Elemental analysis (C, H, O and N) was carried out using an EA 3000 analyzer (EuroVector, Milan, Italy). X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded on a Physical Electronics PHI5700 spectrometer (Chanhassen, MN, USA) using Al Kα radiation (hν =1486.6eV). Binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.6eV. Quantitative analyses of C and O were based on the peak intensities of the C1s and O1s signals, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were obtained from a Rigaku D/Max-rB diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (45kV, 40mA, λ =1.54178Å), the scan range of XRD is 5–50°. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a Pyris1 TGA apparatus (TA Instrument, USA) at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 under a N2 flow of 20mlmin−1. The porous structure was characterized using a N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm at −196°C with an ASAP 2020 instrument (Micromeritics, USA). Before the gas adsorption measurements, the carbon materials were degassed at 200°C under vacuum for 4h. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area was calculated from the N2 adsorption isotherms using the BET equation. The total pore volume was estimated at a relative pressure of 0.01–1.0.

Transmission electron microscopy images of the CNDs were obtained on a JEOL 2011 (FEI, Holland) that was operated at 200kV. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) instrument (Perkin Elmer TV1900, Waltham, MA, USA) from 400cm− 1 to 4000cm− 1 at a resolution of 4cm− 1. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a TU-1900 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 120W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescence microscopy images were obtained using an Axioimager A1 microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The excitation wavelengths were 365, 455, 545nm and the light source for fluorescence microscopy observations was a mercury lamp with a fluorescent filter. All the measurements were performed at room temperature. The morphologies and sizes of the CNDs/TiO2 composites were observed using environmental scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Quanta 200, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA).

The specific conductivity of the solid products was obtained using a four point probe resistivity tester (SDY-4, China). Before testing, the carbon materials were pressed into a wafer using a tablet compressing machine (FY-15, Tianjin, China). The conductivity was estimated from G (S/m) as defined by:
G = 0.1 / R
where R (kΩ/cm) is the bulk resistivity determined directly by the tester.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity and quantum yield test
ce:section.ce:para: A photocatalytic activity test was carried out in a cylindrical quartz photoreactor (275ml) using MB and phenol as the model compound and a 380W Xe lamp (510nm, Shanghai Hualun Lamp Factory, China) positioned inside the reactor as the light source. Photocatalyst powder (0.1g) was added to 100ml aqueous solution (100mg/l). Before the photocatalytic degradation, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30min to establish an adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Samples (5ml) were collected from the suspension and centrifuged. The concentration after illumination was determined spectrophotometrically using a TU-1900 UV–vis spectrometer at 665nm and 270nm respectively.

The quantum yields of the CNDs were calculated using 5μgml− 1 Rhodamine B in ethanol as a standard (quantum yield 90%). The absorbance of Rhodamine B at 543nm was less than 0.05. The calculation was performed according to the following equation: Φu =Φs (Yu/Ys)(As/Au) where u and s represent the CNDs and Rhodamine B, Φ is the quantum yield, and Y is the integrated fluorescence area. A is the absorption at fluorescence excitation.

SEM images of CSs and CS n are shown in Fig. 2 (a)–(d). From (a) it can be seen that the carbon spheres derived from CMC without addition of urea were well dispersed spheres with a smooth surface, and a narrow diameter distribution of 2–3μm. With the addition of urea, for CS0.1 (Fig. 2b), spherical structure with rough outer surfaces and wider diameters of 2–5μm can be obtained, some adhesion was also observed. For CS0.2 (Fig. 2c), significant adhesion of the neighboring spheres can be evidently seen, it exhibited cucurbit shape with a larger and broader size distribution (3–8μm). This can be explained by a Maillard-type reaction where amines react with the carbonyl group of the reducing sugar molecule to form a 1-deoxy-2-amino-1 ketone sugar [18]. Based on the system pH, the reaction can occur through many complex pathways implying that nucleation seeds are formed at different times and of different sizes. Particle growth according to the la Mer model results in larger particles with a heterogeneous size distribution [19]. As the urea mass increased to 0.4g, for CS0.4 (Fig. 2d), the surface of the carbon spheres became rougher and some bulk appeared, indicating surplus of urea, and thus a concentration of 0.5% was an appropriate amount for N doping.

The chemical composition (tested by EA and XPS) of the carbon spheres containing various concentrations of urea is summarized in Table 1 . When the amount of urea increased from 0.1g to 0.4g, the amount of N doped in was linear and varied from 4.42wt% to 6.69wt%. This shows that hydrothermal carbonization is an effective method to prepare N-doped carbon. However, the amount of doped N is not proportional to the urea concentration implying that not all the urea was incorporated. After carbonization at high temperature, CS0.2-550 and CS0.2-900 had higher carbon content while the N concentration decreased dramatically. Some of the N-containing functional groups were volatilized and some were converted into more stable groups after the high temperature treatment.

XPS results showing the nitrogen and oxygen functionalities of CSs, CS0.2, CS0.2-550 and CS0.2-900 are given in Fig. 3 and Table 2 . The CSs are rich in oxygen-containing groups, the binding energies at 531.5, 532.2 and 533.7eV represent CO groups (OI), COH groups and COC groups (O-II), and chemisorbed oxygen (carboxylic groups) or water (O-III), respectively. The O-II groups are the dominant oxygen-containing groups [1,3]. The overall oxygen content decreased for CS0.2 because urea can react with oxygen, especially carbonyl oxygen, this generates nitrogen-containing functional groups. After high temperature activation, the amount of COC and chemisorbed oxygen decreased dramatically because they are unstable at high temperatures. For CS0.2-900, the chemisorbed oxygen disappeared completely.

An obvious wide N peak was observed at 396–403eV. Curve fitting showed that the N peak could be deconvoluted into 4 peaks at 398.1, 399.3, 400.4 and 402.3eV. These represent pyridine (N-6), NH2/NH, pyrrolic/pyridone (N-5), and quaternary (N-Q) nitrogen, respectively [20,21]. The formation of increasingly stable binding states with an increase in activation temperature was evident, from NH2/NH, and pyrrolic/pyridone (N-5) to pyridine and quaternary nitrogen. For CS0.2, the peak centered at 399.3eV is stronger than that of the NH2/NH group peaks, which means that a large amount of amino groups were doped on the surface of CSs. After activation, the intensity of NH2/NH decreased dramatically, and structural N is thus expected to be the main functional group. At higher temperature, the peak at 400.3eV disappeared while the peak intensity of quaternary (N-Q) nitrogen increased. This may have been caused by N groups such as amines being transferred to structural N after activation. Quaternary nitrogen (N-Q) is one of the main N-functionality with more positively charged N, after carbonization at high temperature, they and some protonated pyridinic-N ammonium ions can replace carbon atoms and became part of aromatic/graphene structure [22]. On other word, N-Q structure consist of various form, one possible is protonated pyridinic-N [23]. From the XPS spectra, an obvious chemical shift from 400.7eV to 402.3eV can be seen for the characteristic peak of quaternary (N-Q), confirming such N was protonated, such properties can give samples high electrophilicity, increasing the conductivity of obtained hydrochars.

From Fig. S1 (see Supplementary information), CS0.2-900 maintained its spherical structure after activation at 900°C. However, its carbon diameter decreased from 3–8μm to 1–5μm and this may be caused by the loss of impurities and the erosion of carbon on the surface. The obtained CS0.2-900 was rich in porous structures and it contains randomly oriented disordered mesopores of 2.8nm and micropores of 1.2nm in average size. High-resolution TEM revealed a crystal spacing of 0.37nm, which is more than that of graphite of 0.34nm, and this may be caused by the incorporation of N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and conductivity of the doped materials
ce:section.ce:para: TG curves of CSs, CS0.2, CS0.2-550 and CS0.2-900 are shown in Fig. S2 (see Supplementary information). It is clear that their thermal stability decreases as follows: CS0.2-900>CS0.2-550>CS0.2 >CSs. From the XPS analysis, N exists in the form of N-containing groups and these are more stable than the oxygen-containing groups in carbon. After thermal treatment, the carbon structure is highly developed and contains aromatic structures such as pyridine and quaternary nitrogen, and CS0.2-900 thus has the most stable properties.

The conductivity of the prepared carbons exhibited the same trend as shown in Table 4 . The CSs had a relatively low conductivity even upon activation at 550°C (2.2S/m). After carbonization at 900°C its conductivity increased to 95S/m without any modification. This is caused by a rearrangement of the structure wherein a cohesive aromatic and graphitic-like system was created at high temperature. This is the main factor leading to its good conductivity. On the other hand, carbonization can increase the size of the crystalline phase and the grain as well the amount of delocalized electrons while resistance toward electrical conduction decreases leading to high specific conductivity [25]. N-doping had an obvious and effective influence on structure as determined by XPS and XRD, as a result, increasing the conductivity of obtained hydrochars. N-containing functional groups exhibit basic properties, which can induce electron-donor properties [26]. They replaced C within the aromatic ring structure in the form of pyridine and quaternary nitrogen and lead to an additional electron in the delocalized π-system [27], although N does not act as catalytic activity center, their doping can increase the density of the charge carriers around the C effectively [6,28]. The hydrochar’s electronic band structure hence was influenced [29], which contributes toward higher conductivity, as shown in Table 4. The conductivity of CS n -900 is much higher than that of CSs-900 while CS0.2-900 exhibits the highest electrical conductivity of 166S/m. As the N-doping concentration increases further this value decreases and this can be explained by competition between two independent processes that takes place during the incorporation of nitrogen into the graphite structure. In addition to increasing the amount of electrons, N also leads to disorder in the graphite lattice. Therefore, the maximum conductivity is observed at an intermediate nitrogen concentration and this was 1.94wt% in our case. Overall, the results confirmed that higher conductivity need aromatic and graphitic-like structure, as well appropriate doping amount of N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Texture and surface properties of the CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images and histograms of the size distribution of the CNDs are given in Fig. 5 . This figure shows that the CNDs are spherical and well dispersed. A count of more than 100 CNDs gave a mean diameter of 30.2nm. The crystalline nature of the CNDs was examined by HRTEM and lattice planes with a spacing of 0.34nm were observed in the crystalline structures. This is similar to that of the (002) diffraction plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [30].

XPS was carried out to determine the chemical composition of the CNDs. Three dominant peaks: C1s (284.8eV), N1s (399.4eV) and O1s (531.9eV) were observed suggesting the presence of C (71.5%), O (28.1%) and N (0.4%) in the CNDs, as shown in Fig. S3(a) (see Supplementary information). The N1s peak at 399.4eV is weak and far smaller than that expected from the solid product content. This peak indicates that the nitrogen is mostly in the form of CN [16]. The high C content confirms that a condensed material was produced. The C1s spectra were fit to four peaks (Fig. S3b) at 285.3, 285.7, 286.7 and 289.4eV, which demonstrates that the CNDs were functionalized with CC CC, CN CH, CO and OC groups [3,14]. These results indicate the presence of an aromatic structure and abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface.

FTIR analysis (Fig. S3c) indicated the presence of additional functional groups on the surface of the CNDs. A carbonyl group (CO) band at 1705cm−1, a CO peak at 1000–1400cm−1, and a OH band at 3370–3237cm−1 appeared, indicating the presence of hydrophilic groups. The high amount of oxygen-containing groups can explain the good water-solubility of the CNDs [31]. Condensed CC groups were present as shown by the band at 1667cm−1 with aromatic CH out of plane bending vibrations at 875–750cm−1 indicating the presence of aromatic rings. These results agree with the XPS analysis. A broad absorption around 3100cm−1 is assigned to NH vibration bands, the peaks in 1405cm−1 is characteristic of the amide III CN stretch, suggesting the presence of amino-containing functional groups [31]. The reductive OH or CHO groups that are covalently bound to the carbon framework improve the hydrophilicity and the stability of the CNDs [32].

UV–vis and PL spectra were used to investigate the optical properties of the CNDs. The aqueous solution containing CNDs were light yellow with good dispersibility (insert of Fig. 6 a). A weak and broad peak for UV–vis absorption spectrum was shown around 250–300nm, which is attributed to a complex electron transition on the surface: the π conjugated aromatic system and the n–π* transition of the carbonyl and other oxygen-containing groups [33,34]. The light yellow aqueous solution exhibits bright green luminescence upon 365nm UV irradiation and this is observable with naked eyes, as shown in Fig. 6d. A broad emission centered at 450nm was observed in the emission spectra with an excitation at 365nm. An obvious peak centered at 365nm appeared in the excitation spectrum with an emission wavelength of 450nm (Fig. 6b). PL spectra in Fig. 6c show that the maximum emission wavelength and intensity have a direct relationship with the excitation wavelength, and this is a PL characteristic of carbon dots [31]. Upon changing the excitation wavelength from 360nm to 500nm, the emission peak red-shifts from 453nm to 588nm with a gradual decrease in intensity. The PL properties can be attributed to surface energy traps formed by an incomplete reaction and the radiative recombination of electrons and holes [36]. Additionally, polycyclic aromatic compounds within the CNDs may be responsible for the excellent PL properties [37]. And, emission spectra can be detected when the CNDs samples were excitation by both UV and visible light. We used 365nm as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments. The quantum yield was calculated to be about 18% using Rhodamine B in ethanol as the standard and the yield was found to be higher than that of most reported CNPs [14,15].

Fig. S4 (see Supplementary information) shows optical fluorescence microscope images of the CNPs. The samples were prepared by placing a drop of solution on a glass slip and then covering with a cover glass. The CNPs exhibited different emission colors including blue, green and red when excited at 365nm, 455nm and 545nm, respectively. Many carbon nanomaterials exhibit this phenomenon and this may come from the different surface defect properties under various excitation wavelengths [38].

The relationship between the emission spectra and the pH of the CNDs was studied using a sodium bicarbonate and sodium carbonate buffer solution. The colors ranged from light yellow to deep yellow as the pH was varied from 4 to 10 (Fig. S5b). This may have been caused by a surface property change and the CNDs electronic transitions. With an increase in pH the intensity of the emission peak decreased without a change in wavelength, as reported previously [35]. Oxygen-containing groups on the surface impart acid properties while a high pH changes the solution environment. The negative charge density increases on the surface and influences the π-th* and n–e* electronic transition as well as the chemical properties and surface defects on the CNDs, which leads to fluorescence quenching. A linear calibration plot was obtained with a high correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.95 and this indicates a potential wastewater pH measurement application.

Because CNDs can be excited by UV and by visible light from 300nm to 550nm, they can potentially be used as photocatalyst modifiers. TiO2 is a widely used photocatalyst for the removal of environmental pollution as it is a good oxidant, is highly photocatalytic, chemically stable and has low toxicity. The major obstacle to its effective use is its narrow spectral response range because only UV irradiation is effective, which comprises 5% of incident sunlight. The CNDs investigated here are rich in oxygen-containing groups and are easily attached in a stable manner to the surface of TiO2 [15]. The CNDs were thus combined with TiO2 in a composite system as shown in Fig. 7 (a). HRTEM (inset) confirmed that CNDs of around 30nm were attached to the surface of TiO2 successfully.

Photocatalytic activity was evaluated by the catalyst’s ability to decompose MB under visible light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Compared with pure CNDs and TiO2 as catalysts for MB degradation we found a less than 17% target reduction, and this was mainly because of adsorption. The CNDs/TiO2 composite gave efficient catalysis and 97.1% MB degradation, indicating an interaction between the CNDs and TiO2. The CNDs-0/TiO2 composite without N-doping also exhibited a good photocatalytic effect to give around 95.7% MB degradation, which is a little lower than that of the CNDs/TiO2 composite. This indicates that N weakly affects visible light catalysis, which is the main reason for the induction of electron transitions under visible light irradiation in CNDs. The proposed mechanism is as follows (Fig. 7c): CNDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups are sensitized and upon irradiation by visible light they act as a photosensitizer and absorb photons. Photons with adequate energy excite electrons in the HOMO and they transfer to the LUMO of the CNDs. Free electrons as well energy can then be transferred to the conduction band of TiO2 [39]. These electrons then react with oxidants (O2) adsorbed on TiO2 to produce active oxygen radicals (O2−), which further degrades MB into small molecules.

To confirm the nano quantum dots work actually as photosensitizer, another colorless model of phenol solution was used as shown in Fig. S6. Eliminate the color interferences of model, the photocatalytic performance still perfect. It can been seen that although phenol are larger molecule, still 85.4% can be degraded via CNDs/TiO2 system in three hours, confirming CNDs worked as photosensitizers in the system.

We investigated an efficient way to convert biomass into higher value materials. Solid hydrochar and water-soluble nanodots were obtained by one-pot HTC of CMC using urea as nitrogen source. Urea can react with carbonyl, and present in the form of amidogen, imido groups, pyridine (N-6), pyrrolic/pyridone (N-5) and quaternary (N-Q) nitrogen. Activation at higher temperature contributes to generation of pyridine, quaternary nitrogen with graphite-like structure, higher thermal stability and electrical-conductivity. CNDs are spherical particles with excellent photoluminescence, excitation-dependence and pH-sensitivity abilities. CNDs can act as an effective photosensitizer in photocatalyst system, spectral response range of the CNDs/TiO2 can be expanded to visible light region, 97.1% MB degradation under visible irradiation can be achieved. Overall, this study suggests hydrothermal method is a promising approach for co-production of hydrochar and nanodots from biomass.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Technology R&D Program (2012BAD32B04), the Research Fund for the Fundamental Research Funds of the Central Universities (2572014EB01 and 2572014AB17), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31170545) and Hei Long Jiang Postdoctoral Foundation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2014.12.089.
